name: Under Burro Creek Bridge
short_description: Free BLM rockhounding site near Lake Havasu with excellent agate and chalcedony specimens found in washes and alongside Burro Creek. Perfect beginner-friendly collecting with no permits required.
description:
Under Burro Creek Bridge offers easy access to high-quality agate and chalcedony specimens in the remote desert landscape of northwestern Arizona. The site sits along a productive wash system where seasonal water flow has concentrated and polished specimens over millennia. Collectors regularly find banded agates, colorful chalcedony, and jasper fragments scattered across the wash bed and adjacent terraces. The area benefits from minimal traffic and excellent specimen quality, making it ideal for both beginners and experienced rockhounders. Access is free on BLM land with no permits required - just follow standard personal use collection rules.

The Burro Creek drainage has been a productive collecting area for decades, known among Arizona rockhounders for consistent agate finds and minimal competition. Water-worked specimens here show the characteristic smooth surfaces and vibrant banding patterns that make desert agates so prized. The moderate slope of the terrain makes walking easy, though the rocky wash bottom requires sturdy footwear. Early morning visits are best to avoid afternoon heat and locate specimens before sun glare obscures them.

This location works particularly well for family groups and beginners because specimens are visible and accessible without technical skills or specialized equipment. The creek bed offers natural sorting that concentrates heavier specimens in specific areas. Pack plenty of water, wear sun protection, and bring a small pick or digging tool for extracting partially buried pieces from the wash.
history: Burro Creek sits within the Basin and Range province of northwestern Arizona, where Tertiary-age volcanic and alluvial deposits rich in silica created ideal conditions for agate and chalcedony formation. The creek's active and ancient wash systems have weathered surrounding mineralized formations for millions of years, concentrating attractive specimens along drainage channels that rockhounders continue to work today.
tips: Focus your search in the main wash bed under and downstream from the bridge area, where water flow concentrates heavier specimens. Look for banded agate showing contrasting tan, white, and rust colors - these pieces often appear polished and partially buried in the sandy wash bottom. Bring a small pry bar or rock hammer to extract specimens stuck in clay layers; early morning light helps spot translucent chalcedony before the sun creates harsh shadows. Check along the outer banks of wash bends where water flow has deposited the heaviest material.
terrain_notes: The site sits at 1922 feet elevation with moderate slope terrain typical of the lower Sonoran Desert. The approach follows Box Canyon Wash with rocky, uneven footing requiring sturdy hiking boots. Vegetation is sparse desert scrub with scattered creosote bushes. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically under 0.5 miles. The wash bottom alternates between sandy stretches and rocky sections with sharp volcanic fragments.
best_season: October - April. The Mohave Desert location experiences extreme summer heat (often exceeding 110 degrees Fahrenheit July-August), making winter and spring months ideal for comfortable collecting. Monsoon flash floods are possible in summer, and the creek can flow during these rare events.
written_directions: From Lake Havasu City, drive south on Arizona 95 for approximately 30 miles toward Parker. Turn west onto Burro Creek Road and follow it approximately 13 miles to the bridge crossing. The site is accessible by passenger vehicle on well-maintained dirt road. Park in pullout areas near the bridge structure and walk downstream into the main wash system.
road_conditions: Burro Creek Road is a well-maintained dirt road suitable for standard passenger vehicles. No high-clearance or 4WD required. Occasional washboard surface but passable year-round.
parking_notes: Informal pullout areas available near Burro Creek Bridge. Parking is unpaved dirt with capacity for 3-5 vehicles. No facilities present.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection or selling of specimens allowed. No motorized excavation or digging equipment. Rockhounds must pack out all waste and leave the site in natural condition.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Pack out all trash
Leave site in natural condition
Respect private property boundaries nearby
nearest_services: Lake Havasu City, 51 miles north, has gas, restaurants, hotels, and full services. Parker, Arizona is approximately 40 miles south with basic services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to moderate slope terrain with uneven rocky footing. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots essential due to sharp volcanic rock and loose stone. Walking distance from parking to wash is short but requires navigation across rocky desert. Not suitable for persons with limited mobility or significant balance issues.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find at Under Burro Creek Bridge in Arizona?
faq.answer: This BLM site is known for excellent agate specimens featuring banded patterns in tan, white, and rust colors. You will also find chalcedony, jasper, and quartz. Many pieces are water-polished and visible on the wash surface, making them accessible to beginners. Specimens range from small thumb-sized pieces to larger palm-sized agates.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Burro Creek near Lake Havasu?
faq.answer: No permit is required. This is free BLM public land open to recreational rockhounding under personal use rules. You do not need to pay any fee or obtain advance authorization. Simply follow the 25-pound daily limit and pack out all waste.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to collect agate at Under Burro Creek Bridge?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer temperatures exceed 110 degrees Fahrenheit, making collecting dangerous and uncomfortable. Winter and spring provide comfortable conditions and better visibility of specimens. Avoid monsoon season (July-August) when flash floods are possible in the wash.

faq.question: Is Under Burro Creek Bridge beginner-friendly for family rockhounding?
faq.answer: Yes, this is an excellent beginner and family site. Access is easy, specimens are visible on the surface and in shallow wash beds, and no specialized equipment is required. Children as young as 6 can participate with adult supervision. The short walk to productive areas and casual collecting make it perfect for families.

faq.question: What tools should I bring to find agate at Burro Creek, Arizona?
faq.answer: A small rock hammer, pry bar, or digging tool helps extract specimens partially buried in clay layers of the wash. A hand lens is useful for examining chalcedony for color and quality. Bring sturdy hiking boots due to sharp volcanic rock, plenty of water for desert conditions, and sun protection. A 5-gallon bucket helps transport your daily 25-pound limit.

faq.question: How far is Burro Creek from Lake Havasu City and how do I get there?
faq.answer: The site is approximately 51 miles south of Lake Havasu City. Drive south on Arizona 95 for 30 miles, then turn west onto Burro Creek Road and follow it 13 miles to the bridge. The road is well-maintained dirt suitable for regular cars. Total drive time is about 1.5 hours from Lake Havasu City.

meta_title: Burro Creek Bridge Agate Rockhounding - Arizona
